articles.title: Clinical presentation and outcomes in non-maltophilia Stenotrophomonas spp. bloodstream infections
articles.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 06 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-59102-9 Clinical presentation and outcomes in non-maltophilia Stenotrophomonas spp. bloodstream infections
articles.content:
Abstract

Stenotrophomonas maltophilia complex is an important nosocomial cause of bacteraemia. The complex comprises multiple species, but their relative clinical and genomic significance remains unclear. We examined clinical and genomic differences between S. maltophilia and non-S. maltophilia species to better understand their virulence and implications for patient care. Hospitalized adult patients (≥ 21 years) between January 2022 and June 2024 with Stenotrophomonas species bacteraemia were examined. Clinical data were extracted from electronic medical records. Whole-genome sequencing was performed on all isolates, with species assignment confirmed by average nucleotide identity. S. maltophilia and non-S. maltophilia species were compared. Fifty-three S. maltophilia complex isolates were identified, of which 45 had clinical data. S. maltophilia predominated (n = 32), followed by S. pavanii (n = 7), S. sepilia (n = 4), S. muris (n = 1), and S. geniculata (n = 1). S. maltophilia isolates formed multiple clades with diverse sequence types, consistent with both clonal expansion and ongoing diversification. Virulence genes, including stmPr, smoR, and iron acquisition-associated genes were conserved across S. maltophilia lineages, but stmPr1 was less frequently observed in non-S. maltophilia. Most infections were line-related. Pneumonia-associated bacteraemia appeared more frequently among with S. maltophilia infections. Polymicrobial bacteraemia appeared more commonly in non-S. maltophilia infections. Mortality was similar between groups, but recurrence of infection within one year occurred only in S. maltophilia. Compared with non-S. maltophilia, S. maltophilia appeared to be associated with pneumonia and recurrent bacteraemia episodes, although mortality was similar. These exploratory findings suggest potential differences within the complex, but require validation in larger studies.

articles.title: Towards million-token context windows: a topology-preserving framework for adaptive transformer sparsification
articles.summary: Scientific Reports, Published online: 06 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41598-026-59160-z Towards million-token context windows: a topology-preserving framework for adaptive transformer sparsification
articles.content:
Abstract

Transformer self-attention and billion-node network analyses share a key limitation: all-to-all evaluation creates an \(O(N^2)\) computational cost. Existing methods address this by either distributing the workload across hardware or substituting recurrent operators. This trades associative recall for efficiency. We present Reduced Interaction Sampling (RIS), a stochastic sparsification framework. RIS computes only a fraction of possible pairwise interactions. By leveraging topological redundancy in real-world networks, RIS separates structural accuracy from computational expense. For example, on the com-LiveJournal graph with 4 million nodes, RIS preserves the degree centrality rank (\(\rho = 0.96\)) while using only 10% of the edges. A partition-based setup, RIS-Structural, identifies twice as many hubs as sliding-window methods under heavy sparsity (1.00% vs 0.50%, \(p=0.033\)). In TinyLlama-1.1B attention tests (0.5k–65k tokens), RIS achieves a geometric reach of about 21k tokens at 65k–outperforming Longformer (\(\approx\)2k) and BigBird (\(\approx\)17k). Window-based models surpass \(10^5\) Cumulative Attention Mass but lose 98% of hub recovery. This shows that dense scalar weights poorly reflect long-range geometric reach. RIS maintains a stable Hub Recall with up to 128 times longer sequences and an edge budget below 0.01%. Stochastic sampling provides a mathematically robust way to scale context architectures without structural collapse.

Similar content being viewed by others

Code availability

The source code for the Reduced Interaction Sampling (RIS) framework, including all benchmark scripts and implementation details, is available as a reproducible code capsule on the Code Ocean platform. This includes the core library ris_core.py, the validation suite, and all necessary dependencies to reproduce the results reported in this study. The executable capsule is publicly available under the Apache License 2.0 via its permanent DOI (https://doi.org/10.24433/CO.9986683.v1) and GitHub repository (https://github.com/santosardr/ris).

articles.title: World Cup 2026: Bosnia advances to next round surrounded by 'Free Palestine' chants
articles.summary: World Cup 2026: Bosnia advances to next round surrounded by 'Free Palestine' chants Bosnia's World Cup run puts 'Free Palestine' chants back in the spotlight as fans hail Edin Dzeko's wartime story Bosnia and Herzegovina's supporters celebrate in the streets at the end of the...
articles.content:
World Cup 2026: Bosnia advances to next round surrounded by 'Free Palestine' chants

Bosnia and Herzegovina's historic run to the Fifa World Cup knockout stage has reignited online attention not only for the team's performances, but also for the pro-Palestine chants that have followed Bosnian supporters throughout the tournament.

After a 3-1 victory over Qatar secured Bosnia's place in the Round of 32, videos of fans chanting "Free Palestine" once again spread widely across social media, with many users drawing connections between Bosnia's own experience of war and genocide in the 1990s and Israel's ongoing genocide in Gaza.

Ahead of the match between Bosnia & Herzegovina and Qatar in Seattle yesterday, Bosnian fans chanted “Palestine” both before kickoff and inside the stadium during the game, showing solidarity with Palestinians at the World Cup.

— The New Arab (@The_NewArab) June 25, 2026

Bosnia and Herzegovina went on to win the match… pic.twitter.com/kecbtpZvLI

The chants have become a recurring feature of Bosnia's World Cup campaign, with supporters heard chanting "Free Palestine" inside stadiums and during fan marches in host cities, earning praise from pro-Palestine audiences online.

For many Bosnians, the solidarity is deeply personal.

Captain Edin Dzeko, widely regarded as the country's greatest-ever footballer, spent his childhood living through the Siege of Sarajevo during the Bosnian War.

Born in Sarajevo in 1986, Dzeko was just six years old when the siege began. In a recent letter published in The Players' Tribune, he recalled growing up surrounded by shelling and sniper fire, making dangerous trips to collect water because his family had none, and finding moments to play football whenever the violence briefly subsided.

His family's apartment was destroyed during the war, forcing 15 members of his extended family to live together in his grandparents' small apartment for years.

Perhaps the most haunting memory Dzeko has shared is of the day his mother refused to let him go and play football with other children. Minutes later, a shell struck the pitch, killing several youngsters. Years later, Dzeko reflected simply: "My mother saved my life."

The 40-year-old made his 150th appearance for Bosnia against Qatar on Wednesday, continuing a career that has become symbolic of the country's journey from war to the world stage.

🥹 Sobrevivió a las bombas de pequeño.

— Jonay Amaro (@AmaroJonay) June 24, 2026

🤱 Su madre evitó que le mataran como a sus amigos, diciendo que no fuera a la plaza de su ciudad, donde iba a jugar al fútbol.

💪 Tiene 40 años, pero ha liderado a Bosnia para volver a un Mundial 12 años después.

🇧🇦 EDIN DZEKO, LEYENDA pic.twitter.com/dhU0mayq3o

The Bosnian War, which lasted from 1992 to 1995, claimed around 100,000 lives and displaced more than two million people. Its final months saw the Srebrenica genocide, in which more than 8,000 Bosniak Muslim men and boys were murdered by Bosnian Serb forces after the UN-declared "safe area" fell.

Bosnians view the country's wartime experience as inseparable from their solidarity with Palestinians.

Comparisons between Bosnia in the 1990s and Gaza have been common in public discourse since Israel launched its military assault on the enclave in October 2023.

Supporters on social media echoed that sentiment following Bosnia's victory, describing the team's success as carrying significance beyond football. Many shared clips of the "Free Palestine" chants alongside images from the Bosnian War, saying a nation that experienced ethnic cleansing and mass atrocities had chosen to stand publicly with Palestinians.

Bosnia fans’ chant for Palestine echoed through the whole stadium as Bosnia and Herzegovina booked their place in the next round of the WC.

— Leyla Hamed (@leylahamed) June 24, 2026

Whatever attempts are made to silence it, solidarity with Palestine keeps finding its way into football’s biggest stages. Even in the U.S. pic.twitter.com/1Ky4ibN4Pm

Bosnia's qualification means those chants are likely to be heard again.

articles.title: Show HN: 1000songs.co.uk – 100 years of music, 10 tracks a year
articles.summary: Hey HN, I built https://1000songs.co.uk , an alternative, hand-curated audio time machine spanning a century of music (1930–2030). The constraint is rigid: exactly 10 songs for every single year. I think it’s much more fun to measure time in songs than in hours, minutes, or se...
articles.content:
Hey HN,

I built https://1000songs.co.uk, an alternative, hand-curated audio time machine spanning a century of music (1930–2030). The constraint is rigid: exactly 10 songs for every single year.

I think it’s much more fun to measure time in songs than in hours, minutes, or seconds. I think of it less as a "playlist" (a word I'm intentionally avoiding) and more as a "break in case of emergency" list. It’s a folk art celebration of how humans discover music through friends, radio, and late-night rabbit holes, rather than cold streaming recommendation vectors.

The Twist: It's an Offline-First Template for Your Own Taste.

While the site hosts my personal archive, I built it to be entirely decentralized and user-owned.

* Create & Keep: You can use the "Curate" tool to map out your own bespoke sonic eras, add any songs you want, and update the collection.

* Download the Entire Site: At the bottom of the page, there is a "DOWNLOAD SITE" button. You can save the entire project as a standalone file to keep forever and run completely offline (obviously, audio previews require an active connection, but the engine and your data live locally).

Curation Logic & The Dataset Every bit of metadata was typed manually, cross-checking facts against Wikipedia and Discogs (so please let me know if you spot any typos!). The tracks are selected purely on personal taste—if I find a song I love more than one currently on the list, I swap it out.

The curation is heavily inspired by decades of British radio.

I still need help! The archive currently stands at 923 tracks. I am hitting a massive bottleneck trying to fill the final slots for the 1930s and 1940s without just defaulting to the most obvious radio hits of the era.

If you have deep-cut suggestions for early jazz, blues, big band, delta folk, or interesting global sounds from 1930–1949, please drop them in the comments.

I’d love to get your feedback on the offline architecture, the UI, or your own philosophies on music preservation!

articles.content: <p>Rowan is an Editorial Associate and Apprentice Writer for TechRadar. A recent addition to the news team, he is involved in generating stories for topics that spread across TechRadar's categories. His interests in audio tech and knowledge in entertainment culture help bring the latest updates in tech news to our readers.</p> <p><br></p> <p>He has been writing for publications since he started his studies at age 18. Rowan graduated from Cardiff University in 2023 after attaining a Master's in Creative Writing, and earlier a Bachelor's in Media, Journalism, and Culture. He began his journey as a writer at Cardiff University's Quench Magazine contributing to film/ TV, music, and culture sections, later becoming Music Section Editor. </p> <p><br></p> <p>In his free time, Rowan is a freelance writer for Cardiff-based culture magazine Buzz where he reviews music, film, and conducts interviews with featured guests. When he is not writing, you can find him at any given music gig, or endlessly scrolling TikTok immersing in celebrity news and drama.  </p>

articles.title: A solution to RAM-aggedon? AMD buys startup to transform SSDs into cheap 'virtual RAM' to cut price, its third attempt at solving memory conundrum after RAMdisk and StoreMI
articles.summary: AMD's latest AI-centric acquisition could be a game-changer for its data center ambitions
articles.content: <p>Rahim Amir is a UAE-based tech writer who enjoys building PCs as much as he enjoys writing about them. He has been professionally writing about PC hardware since 2023, focusing on buyer’s guides, hardware reviews, and sponsored content and features related to tech.<br><br>Having built hundreds of gaming PCs and being an avid gamer in his spare time, Rahim tends to have stronger opinions about hardware than most. This is particularly on display when he gets his way with powerful, but minimalistic RGB builds even as Small Form Factor (SFF) PCs come a close second.<br><br>In addition to his contributions to TechRadar, Rahim’s work has also been featured on Game Rant and financial news websites.<br><br>When he’s not working, you can find him playing DotA with friends or schmoozing to take the world over in Civilization. Alternatively, you can find him binging through the entirety of the Lord of The Rings universe with extended editions in play where applicable.<br><br>You can currently catch Rahim grinding Path of Exile 2, complaining about his (extremely low) unique loot drop rate, or actively participating in one of the numerous (and heated) debates centered around Tolkien's universe on multiple forums daily.<br><br>If you have a PC build or a Satisfactory playthrough in progress, he is likely to have some advice to send your way, especially regarding verticality being key for the latter. For the former, Rahim enjoys all aspects of the process including researching the components he will eventually use, benchmarking the latest and greatest hardware he can get his hands on, and somewhat surprisingly, cable management once he gets his latest build to POST.</p>

articles.title: CenterPoint Energy clears a fresh all-time high, shares near record levels in NYSE trading
articles.summary: CenterPoint Energy (CNP) has reached a new all-time high of $44.48 on the NYSE, driven by steady earnings, reiterated guidance, and a long-standing dividend payment history. The stock is currently trading just under this peak, reflecting resilient demand in the utilities secto...
articles.content:
By Daniel Hoffmann, Chart & Technicals desk. Reviewed prior to publication on 2026-06-25, 21:00.

CenterPoint Energy (US15189T1079) set a new all-time high at 44.48 US dollars this week as the shares trade on the NYSE in the multi-utilities segment.An Investing.com report highlights the record mark and recent performance data.
The stock recently traded at around 43.8 to 44.4 dollars, leaving it only a small distance below the fresh peak.

What the latest high shows

The 44.48 dollar print puts CenterPoint Energy shares roughly 23.6 percent higher than a year ago, according to recent performance figures in the utilities sector.Investing.com notes the 1-year total return alongside the record.
At around 44.46 dollars, the stock trades about 1 percent below the 52-week high of 44.47 dollars, indicating resilient demand in a defensive industry.
The move comes after CenterPoint Energy reported first-quarter 2026 earnings per share of 0.56 dollars, slightly below a 0.57 dollar forecast, but with revenue of 2.95 billion dollars exceeding expectations around 2.9 billion dollars.The same Investing.com coverage points to the revenue beat and reiterated guidance.

Chart levels and utilities context

Recent quote data from June 24 show CenterPoint Energy shares closing near 43.89 dollars after trading between 43.31 and 43.95 dollars, with the NYSE listing carrying moderate daily volatility for a regulated utility name.MarketBeat provides the latest price range and sector classification.
Intraday quotes around 43.77 dollars on June 25 suggest only a narrow consolidation below the record, with a percentage change of about minus 0.25 percent on that snapshot.Financialdata.net lists the 43.77 dollar trade and intraday change.
In the wider utilities peer group, CenterPoint competes with other US electric and gas names, where steady dividends and regulated returns often drive investor interest rather than sharp price swings.

The business behind the record

CenterPoint Energy’s core business is energy delivery rather than generation, with operations spanning electric transmission and distribution, natural gas distribution and related energy services across several US states including Texas, Indiana, Ohio, Louisiana, Minnesota and Mississippi.Power Choice Texas describes the company’s role in the Houston-area transmission and distribution system.
In the greater Houston and Galveston metro area, CenterPoint operates the poles-and-wires electricity network, handling meter reads and service calls, with delivery charges accounting for roughly 30 percent of residential electricity bills in that region.
The company’s long history of more than 140 years and a track record of 56 consecutive years of dividend payments with a yield around 2.1 percent underpin its appeal to income-oriented investors in the utilities sector.Investing.com highlights the dividend streak and current yield.

Where the shares trade today

The CenterPoint Energy shares (US15189T1079) trade on the NYSE under the ticker CNP, with recent quotes around 43.77 US dollars as of 2026-06-25, 12:11, reflecting the latest intraday trading snapshot in New York.
This price level keeps the utilities stock only modestly below the newly marked all-time high and within a tight recent range on the US exchange.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute investment advice, investment recommendation or an offer or solicitation to buy or sell any financial instrument. All data are based on sources deemed reliable but cannot be guaranteed. Investors should conduct their own research or consult a qualified financial advisor before making investment decisions.

articles.title: Polestar Barred From Selling Vehicles in U.S.: What It Means for Consumers
articles.summary: The U.S. government has barred Swedish EV brand Polestar from selling its China-built vehicles in the U.S. for the 2027 model year and beyond due to national security risks associated with connected vehicles from China or Russia. Polestar, which is owned by Chinese conglomerat...
articles.content: The U.S. government has barred Swedish EV brand Polestar from selling its China-built vehicles in the U.S. for the 2027 model year and beyond due to national security risks associated with connected vehicles from China or Russia. Polestar, which is owned by Chinese conglomerate Geely Holding, will continue to sell its existing stock of Polestar 3 and 4 vehicles and support current customers, but its future in the U.S. market is effectively over. This ruling, combined with existing challenges like tariffs and lower EV demand, is expected to further depress the resale value of Polestar models in the U.S.
